findings.problem: The chapter replays the same Peter-control beat three times (hospital warning, home timetable, written house rules) with limited leverage change, so scene energy plateaus instead of escalating consequence. This undercuts causal momentum for the chapter's spine conflict.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rebuild the confrontation sequence across snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:103-147, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:217-241, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:289-303 so each pass introduces a new control mechanism and forces a distinct counter-move from Noreen (e.g., hospital contact designation fight, transport/key choke point at home, then document/control breach). Keep one beat informational and make the other two action-consequence beats.
findings.acceptance_test: PASS if, when reading the revised three confrontation spans (103-147, 217-241, 289-303), each ends with a different concrete state change (not just repeated policy language): one institutional, one household-logistical, and one personal-documentary; FAIL if any two beats resolve with functionally the same argument and no new consequence.

findings.problem: Sanitization drift: after building institutional gatekeeping pressure, the draft jump-cuts from hospital planning to post-discharge home return without rendering the discharge confrontation itself. Summary replaces scene at the exact point the chapter's institutional wound should be embodied.
findings.rewrite_direction: Insert an on-page discharge beat between snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:193 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:195 that stages the actual policy handoff (who controls updates/visits/milk transport), Noreen's attempted agency, and the immediate cost of compliance or refusal. Keep it in close third with body-state detail rather than administrative summary.
findings.acceptance_test: PASS if the revised transition includes a dramatized discharge interaction in which (1) a staff actor states a concrete restriction, (2) Noreen makes a specific request or refusal, and (3) an immediate practical consequence is shown before she reaches home; FAIL if discharge remains off-page summary.

findings.problem: Timidity: the first explicit in-house coercion contract is defused by quick retreat ("she was tired and went upstairs") instead of being played through as embodied conflict. The prose steps back from the emotional and physical reality the premise has already established.
findings.rewrite_direction: Expand snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:291-303 into a full pressure exchange with at least one attempted enforcement move by Peter and one physically grounded refusal beat from Noreen (pain, balance, blood-loss fatigue, fear management), so refusal has visible cost in-scene.
findings.acceptance_test: PASS if the revised 291-303 span contains at least one action attempt by Peter to operationalize the rule set and one immediate embodied response from Noreen that changes what happens next; FAIL if refusal is still resolved by summary exit without enacted pushback.

findings.problem: Mechanical repetition from over-literal style-bible token reuse: Peter's dialogue leans on near-continuous administrative-control diction (order, review, arrangements, restrictions), which flattens his voice into one-note procedure instead of dynamic coercive rhetoric.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise Peter's lines in snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:217-299 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:367-377 to preserve low-contraction formal control while varying tactic and rhythm: mix one clipped threat, one socially coded shame lever, and one brittle emotional slip so his control voice evolves under stress.
findings.acceptance_test: PASS if, in the revised Peter spans (217-299 and 367-377), at least one line departs from procedural phrasing into personal pressure and no single control noun family (order/arrangement/system/review/control) dominates more than two lines; FAIL if his speech remains uniformly bureaucratic in every turn.

findings.problem: Recurring prose formula: the chapter repeatedly uses the same 'like a ...' simile template well beyond the allowed repetition threshold, which turns figurative language from choice into tic and softens line-level surprise.
findings.rewrite_direction: In snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:103-207 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:267-411, keep only the strongest one or two similes and recast the rest as direct sensory statements, action verbs, or object-specific observation tied to Noreen's body-state.
findings.acceptance_test: PASS if the revised chapter contains no more than two 'like a/an ...' simile constructions and each remaining simile marks a distinct emotional pivot; FAIL if the same template appears three or more times again.

findings.problem: A 10+ paragraph stretch runs without focalizer interiority, reducing close-third texture to external staging and dialogue exchange. The chapter briefly reads like a screenplay instead of embodied POV prose.
findings.rewrite_direction: Thread Noreen's interior response through snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_15.md:243-267 (sensation, tactical self-assessment, or memory flashes keyed to the Elsie/Peter exchange) without breaking scene tempo.
findings.acceptance_test: PASS if the revised 243-267 span includes at least three distinct interiority beats from Noreen with no gap longer than four consecutive paragraphs without interior access; FAIL if a 10+ paragraph exterior-only run remains.

summary: Chapter 15 lands core stakes and continuity, but it does not clear pass threshold: institutional conflict is partially sanitized by an off-page discharge jump, domestic coercion beats repeat with limited leverage shift, Peter's dialogue becomes mechanically procedural, and prose-level repetition/interiority dropouts reduce texture.
